course_description:
This course develops students' knowledge and applied skills in performing a thorough gross autopsy and selecting appropriate specimens for diagnostic testing. When investigating, an understanding of pathological processes must be achieved, such that a diagnostic plan and appropriate testing can be used to arrive at a diagnostic outcome. Concepts and skills in clinical pathology will also be developed. All training is case-based, as students will work through cases submitted through the Veterinary Laboratory Services as well as materials in scenario-based modules.

Veterinary diagnostic pathology is an important part of clinical training, providing theoretical knowledge and practical skills that are essential to competent practice and professional registration. Anatomic and clinical pathology are key to understanding the pathophysiology of disease processes and to learning appropriate diagnostic skill sets. In this course, students will develop and apply their knowledge and skills in the diagnosis of veterinary disease in both the antemortem and postmortem context and learn the importance of this information for both individual patients and herd health situations.

Instruction in diagnostic pathology will be predominantly through clinical cases performed by the VLS or on rotations/placements, and via self-directed learning opportunities. Students will become competent with in-house clinical pathology analysers, perform and interpret clinical pathology tests (e.g. haematology, biochemistry, urinalysis), and manage clinical autopsy cases. Competency will be assessed through performance assessments and hurdles, completion of a skills list and case logs, autopsy case presentations, and a capstone examination.

Please note that unexpected changes to the Timetable may occur throughout the Semester. Therefore, it is crucial to regularly check the course Blackboard site. Any updates to the timetable will be communicated to students via Blackboard Announcements.
course_aims:
The diagnostic anatomic pathology component aims to enhance students’ abilities to interpret gross pathological and histopathological findings within a practical context, and also submit appropriate samples to laboratories to maximise diagnostic opportunities.

The diagnostic clinical pathology component aims to ensure students are competent and confident in the analysis and interpretation of blood, urine, and cytology samples for core veterinary species.

learning_outcomes.description: Conduct detailed autopsies, identify pathological processes, and analyse samples to diagnose diseases in the core companion and farm animals adhering to principles of biosecurity and public health.

Review disease mechanisms and significance, develop diagnostic plans, create differential diagnosis lists, and provide advice on disease management for individuals and groups. Use both intuitive and analytical clinical reasoning strategies alongside foundational pathophysiological principles for clinical and autopsy case workups.

Interpret clinical pathology results while recognising analytical limitations and sources of error, selecting appropriate diagnostic tests considering proper biosecurity principles throughout the diagnostic process. Develop differential diagnoses as well as identifying and prioritising problems, then integrate clinical and laboratory results to reach a diagnosis. Understand key laboratory workflows and how pre-analytical and analytical factors influence result quality.

Communicate effectively about diagnostic pathology. Maintain professionalism in interactions and actively engage in feedback to assess strengths, address weaknesses, and develop skills for professional readiness.

assessment_details.title: Diagnostic Pathology Performance
assessment_details.due_date: Assessed during the three week diagnostic pathology rotation.
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited, Work integrated learning, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: The 3 week diagnostic pathology rotation is predominantly oriented around clinical cases presented to Veterinary Laboratory Services (VLS). Students will become competent with in-house clinical pathology analysers, perform and interpret clinical pathology tests (e.g. haematology, biochemistry, urinalysis), and manage clinical autopsy cases. Students will receive formative feedback throughout the rotation. Details of shift start and end times and after hours duty rosters can be found on the VETS5020 course Blackboard site prior to the start of each rotation. (1) Diagnostic Pathology Performance Assessment The diagnostic pathologists will provide a minimum of THREE (3) student performance feedback forms during the rotation. The marking rubric is available on the VETS5020 course Blackboard site. Completion of the performance assessment includes submission of the Student Pathology Report. Students MUST provide a written report for all autopsies. On completion, the report must be emailed by no later than 10am the next business day to the supervising pathologist for review. The supervising pathologist may require multiple editorial changes to the report to achieve a satisfactory final outcome. The final version must then be uploaded to the relevant student group on the VETS5020 course Blackboard site. Case Log Data Upload Information (the case log data) pertinent to every case must be uploaded to the spreadsheet provided to each student group and emailed to the supervising pathologist by 10am of the next business day. The final version of the student pathology report MUST be uploaded to the corresponding group the student belongs to in the VETS5020 Blackboard site. The Performance Feedback Form will comprise five (5) equally weighted categories (Knowledge base; Clinical skills; Technical skills; Professional skills, and Communication). Each category has three performance marks (0/Does not meet standard; 1/Meets standard; 2/Exceeds standard). The rubric has a total of 10 possible marks. An average of all Diagnostic Pathology Performance Assessment marks is calculated at the end of the rotation. (2) Direct Observation of Procedural Skills (DOPS) During the Diagnostic Rotation, students must complete a minimum of TWO (2) DOPS . One small animal autopsy - dog or cat Second autopsy - dog, cat, large animal, wildlife or exotic Instructions for the various procedures and their assessment rubrics are available in My Knowledge Map (MKM). Students will receive feedback and an overall rating on the various composite skills within the DOPS. Each skill must be performed to an overall assessment of "Meets Standard and is Day-One Ready" in order to pass this assessment task. (3) Diagnostic Pathology Skills During the rotation, students will be assessed on the following diagnostic pathology skills: Removal of a brain with a handsaw (any species) Dissection of the heart using UQ standard methodology Presentation of a case at pathology gross rounds, emphasising case history and lesion description. Collection of a histopathology or other diagnostic sample (bacteriology, virology, toxicology, parasitology) Demonstrated preparation of a sample for cytological assessment (fine needle aspirate, tissue smear, squash prep etc. with Diff-Quik or Giemsa staining) These compound skills are specific to VETS5020 and must be signed off by a pathologist/tutor during the rotation. Completion of these items will be graded as P ass/Fail . Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Students will submit the following items during their rotation through MyKnowledge Map (ePad):
The required DOPS - completed to "Meets Standard" The Diagnostic Pathology Skills
Students will submit the following items during their rotation through Blackboard :
Student Pathology Report Case Log Data Upload
The Pathologist will complete and submit the 'Diagnostic Pathology Performance Assessment.'
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Students with acceptable reasons (medical or compassionate reasons or as a result of exceptional circumstances) may apply for an extension of the deadline. Information on applying for an extension can be found here: my.UQ Applying for an extension .
Extension applications must be received by the assessment due date and time.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension .

assessment_details.title: Autopsy Case Study Presentation
assessment_details.due_date: Performed during the scheduled Diagnostic Pathology rotation
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited, Work integrated learning. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: During the rotation, each student will choose an in-depth case study, which will be presented orally in rounds format. If the case load is insufficient, it may be necessary for students to share cases. Additional details and the marking rubric will be available on the VETS5020 course Blackboard site. Use of Artificial Intelligence This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. assessment_details.title: Cytology Slide Review
assessment_details.due_date: Submissions are due prior to the scheduled three hour tutorial in the final week of the diagnostic pathology rotation.
assessment_details.task_description: This is a structured, case-based cytology learning experience. Students will have access to 15 scanned (online) cytology slides. In each week of the diagnostic pathology rotation, students are expected to spend three hours working independently to review FIVE (5) slides. To facilitate systematic appraisal, a structured template will be available on the VETS5020 course Blackboard site. In Week 3 of the rotation, all 15 slides will be reviewed during a three hour tutorial (face to face and Zoom) in which students may ask questions and extend their understanding of cytological pathology. Students must submit, to the VETS5020 course Blackboard site, a completed template for each of the 15 slides prior to the final week tutorial. All constituent components of the template must be adequately addressed, but a diagnosis is NOT required. The objective of this task is to emphasise the necessity of a systematic approach to reviewing cytology. The assessment is Pass/Fail , and therefore a hurdle. To pass this hurdle, all 15 templates must be graded as satisfactory. Templates NOT graded as satisfactory will be returned for further work and then resubmitted. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submissions will be uploaded to the relevant student group on the VETS5020 course Blackboard site.

assessment_details.title: Capstone Examination
assessment_details.due_date: Second End of Semester Exam Period 7/11/2026 - 21/11/2026
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: The Capstone Examination will provide a comprehensive evaluation of students’ clinical problem-solving skills, regardless of species or discipline. The exam assesses core knowledge, clinical reasoning, and the ability to diagnose and manage conditions commonly encountered during the first year of practice. The exam will include contributions from the following courses: VETS5015, VETS5020, VETS5024, VETS5028, VETS5029, and VETS5030 . Exam Structure Two parts, each containing 84 multiple-choice (MCQ) questions Each part will take approximately two hours Questions from each course will be weighted according to the course’s unit value For sample questions, exam duration details, and the distribution of questions per section, please refer to your course Blackboard site. Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.exam_details: Planning time 10 minutes Duration 120 minutes Calculator options Any calculator permitted Open/closed book Closed book examination - no written materials permitted Exam platform Inspera Invigilation Invigilated in person
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Assessment using the Inspera platform
Students must click the ‘SUBMIT NOW’ button on the summary page to submit their answers. After submission, a confirmation will appear on the dashboard indicating that the answers have been submitted and can no longer be edited.
For all exams lasting 60, 90, 120, or 180 minutes, an additional 10 minutes of planning time is provided to read the questions and plan responses, though students may start working immediately if they choose.
It’s crucial to ensure your laptop meets the device requirements for Inspera assessments before the exam. If you don’t have a suitable laptop, you can borrow one from the Library. Check Get Familiar with Inspera for the most up-to-date information about using the platform. Note that power outlets will not be available during exams.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to defer this exam .
Students must be available for their examinations on the scheduled date, time, and campus of their course enrolment. The exam format is determined by the Course Coordinator and may include written (paper-based, online, or digital), practical, oral, or other timed assessments (such as quizzes).
If you are unable to sit for your original examination, you may be eligible for a deferred examination. To apply, you must demonstrate that unavoidable circumstances prevented you from attending your exam. If this is not possible, you can apply for a one-off discretionary deferred exam. All requests for deferred examinations MUST include supporting documentation. For more information, including acceptable evidence and application instructions, please visit my.UQ Deferring an Exam .
Please note that there are no provisions to defer an already-deferred exam. You must be available to sit your deferred examination at the allocated time. Students who submit five or more deferral requests within a twelve-month period will be contacted with advice on the services and support available within the University.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam .

learning_activities.topic: Autopsies During the three-week diagnostic pathology rotation, students will perform autopsies on submitted specimens for disease diagnosis. Students will engage in collection and submission of appropriate samples, as well as in writing pathology reports. Sub-activity: Autopsy
